perturbation.original_text: Company shall provide and maintain, at its own expense, commercial general liability insurance and advertising injury coverage, with limits of not less than One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00), and shall cause such policy to be endorsed to state that Duval is an additional named insured thereunder. A certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage shall be furnished to Duval within thirty (30) days of the full execution of this Agreement. Such insurance policy shall provide that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such policy or remove Duval as an additional named insured without prior written notice to Duval at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall endeavor to provide commercial general liability insurance and advertising injury coverage, with what they deem as appropriate limits. A certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage shall be furnished to Duval when practically possible. Such insurance policy will attempt to provide that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such policy without providing reasonable notice to Duval.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall provide and maintain' with 'shall endeavor to provide,' making the insurance obligation less definitive. Replacing 'with limits of not less than One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00)' with 'with what they deem as appropriate limits' makes the insurance coverage amount completely discretionary. The phrase 'when practically possible' weakens the requirement to furnish a certificate of insurance, and replacing 'at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof' with 'reasonable notice' creates uncertainty regarding the notification period, which can lead to insufficient notice and increased liability risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 514 mandates that an insurer provide clear and specific terms within an insurance policy, including the amount of coverage. The modification allows the company to provide insurance with 'what they deem as appropriate limits,' which circumvents the law's requirement for a clearly defined coverage amount and sets up a subjective standard that can be challenged as not providing real coverage, potentially invalidating the purpose of having Duval named as additional insured.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Insurance Code § 514 by replacing the specific requirement for 'limits of not less than One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00)' with 'what they deem as appropriate limits.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the coverage amount, undermining the clarity and specificity mandated by insurance law, as the Law Explanation describes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to California Insurance Code § 514, although without direct text, the Law Explanation's interpretation of the code's purpose supports that this change introduces a subjective standard.

perturbation.original_text: Licensor agrees to cause Duval to wear Company's Products sold by Company when playing professional golf, and while participating in golf exhibitions and outings, upon the condition that Company supply Duval with such amounts of properly fitting Products, in fabrics and styles approved by Duval and Company, as Duval may reasonably request which are suitable for his use in tournament competition.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensor agrees to encourage Duval to wear Company's Products when playing professional golf, if the company has a sufficient supply. Duval and Company will collaborate on choosing fabrics and styles for the product to be used during tournament competitions.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing 'agrees to cause Duval to wear' with 'agrees to encourage Duval to wear,' weakening the obligation to ensure Duval uses the products. Adding 'if the company has a sufficient supply' makes the obligation contingent on the company's supply, which may be unreliable. The removal of 'such amounts of properly fitting Products, in fabrics and styles approved by Duval and Company, as Duval may reasonably request which are suitable for his use in tournament competition' and replacement with 'Duval and Company will collaborate on choosing fabrics and styles' eliminates the requirement that the supplied products are proper, properly fitted, and suitable for tournament competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. If the Company's products are not suitable for professional tournament use or are not provided in adequate amounts and proper fit, advertising Duval's endorsement could be considered misleading if he is not genuinely using them. The modified language creates a situation where Duval's endorsement might be promoted, even if the products provided don't meet professional standards or are not used regularly, therefore creating a possibility for false advertising by not meeting reasonable endorsement standards.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17500 regarding false advertising. The original text requires the Licensor to ensure Duval wears the Company's products. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'encourage' and makes it contingent on 'sufficient supply'. Furthermore, the original contract requires the products to be 'properly fitting Products...suitable for his use in tournament competition.' This requirement is removed in the changed text. According to the Law Explanation, this weakening and removal 'creates a situation where Duval's endorsement might be promoted, even if the products provided don't meet professional standards or are not used regularly, therefore creating a possibility for false advertising by not meeting reasonable endorsement standards.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'It is unlawful for any person...to make or disseminate...any statement, concerning that real or personal property...to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto' - implying that if the product isn't used as implied through the endorsement (regularly and in professional tournaments), it's a false advertisement.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that it will aggressively promote Duval and his association with Company and Company Products each Contract Year during the Contract Period.
perturbation.changed_text: Company will consider promoting Duval and his association with the Company and Company Products as it deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation to 'aggressively promote' Duval. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'agrees that it will aggressively promote' with 'will consider promoting ... as it deems necessary.' This makes the promotional activity discretionary and subjective, based on the company's assessment of 'necessity', creating ambiguity around the actual marketing commitment.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 45 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. A lack of aggressive promotion, as originally agreed, could be seen as undermining Duval's marketability and the value of his endorsement. If the reduced promotional effort diminishes Duval's commercial value, it could be interpreted as an unfair practice, potentially impacting the golfer's ability to secure other endorsements and therefore impacting market competition. By only 'considering' promotion 'as it deems necessary', the company effectively negates a key element of the endorsement deal, raising questions of fair practice concerning how they are using Duval's name and image.

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates that the company 'will aggressively promote' Duval, which imposes a clear obligation. The modified text weakens this by stating the company 'will consider promoting ... as it deems necessary,' making promotion discretionary. 15 U.S.C. § 45, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits unfair methods of competition. The law explanation posits that reduced promotional efforts could undermine Duval's marketability and constitute an unfair practice. While the snippet does not explicitly define 'unfair methods of competition,' the changed text, by creating ambiguity and potentially diminishing Duval's commercial value, contradicts the implied intent of the law to prevent unfair practices that could impact market competition.
